text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic repair of a four-centimeter umbilical hernia. The omentum is adhered to the hernia sac, and gentle blunt dissection is used to reduce the contents. A small sponge is used to press down the omentum, and harmonic dissection may be employed to release the neck of the hernia sac, ensuring no external contents remain inside.

The surgeon performs dissection to separate adhered omentum using laparoscopic instruments, including bipolar scissors or harmonic devices. The omentum is gradually separated, revealing the hernia sac contents. A dual polyurethane mesh is placed, with its white polypropylene side facing the abdominal wall and the blue polyurethane side facing the bowel, to minimize adhesion risk.

The surgeon stretches the mesh across the hernia site, ensuring no corrugation remains. Sutures are placed subcutaneously, with knots tied beneath the skin. ProTac titanium implants are used for outer and inner crowning to secure the mesh, stabilizing it until fibrosis occurs. The abdominal wall is held perpendicular to the ProTac tip for deeper fixation.

The surgeon carefully fires the ProTac device around the defect, avoiding the inferior epigastric vessel to prevent bleeding. The medial umbilical ligaments are visible, and the ProTac is applied on either side, ensuring not to fire directly over the defect to prevent skin pricking and infection. The inner grounding is completed with additional ProTac applications around the defect's margin.
